text: The laparoscopic view shows the surgeon performing a detorsion of the ilioanal J pouch, using precise instrument manipulation to correct the twisted segment.

Restorative proctocolectomy with ilial pouch anal anastomosis or IPAA, which was first introduced in nineteen seventy eight, has been the surgical treatment of choice for patients with ulcerative colitis who are medically refractory or in those patients with dysplasia. Because of the widespread adoption of laparoscopic surgery, the outcomes for this procedure have been proven to be better than those with the open technique. Even though the technique has improved, complications are still present and can include small bowel obstruction, pouch leakage, anastomotic stricture, fistula formation, and pouchitis, to name a few.

The case presented in this video is of a thirty-seven-year-old female with a history of ulcerative colitis who underwent a three-stage J-pouch procedure one year prior. She presented to the emergency department with abdominal distension, nausea, dull abdominal pain, and inability to pass stool or gas for one day's duration. Of note, the patient underwent stricture dilation two weeks ago. Upon admission, a rectal tube was placed with relief of her pain and distension.

During the diagnostic laparoscopy, the surgeon observes clear free fluid in the abdomen and dilated small bowel extending to the pelvis. The ileoanal pouch is visualized, revealing a 360-degree twist involving the uterus and its mesentery. The bowel appears uncompromised.

An additional trocar is placed to assist with grasping, allowing the surgeon to gently manipulate and detorse the J-pouch. The J-pouch is observed in correct positioning without twisting, and the mesentery shows no intrinsic twisting. The surgeon examines deeper into the pelvis to confirm proper positioning.

The surgeon lyses adhesions between the distal portion of the J-pouch and the pelvis using sharp dissection. The presacral space appears large and empty, potentially contributing to the twisting of the J-pouch. After detorsion, the surgeon runs the small bowel with gentle grasping, confirming no additional obstructions. The transition point between dilated and collapsed bowel is identified, and the J-pouch is repositioned to its neutral position in the pelvis.

The surgeon performs a rigid sigmoidoscopy to confirm the detorsion of the pouch. The mucosa appears normal, and a slight stricture at the anastomosis is gently dilated to facilitate the passage of the sigmoidoscope.

The surgeon desufflates the J-pouch and performs a pexy to the pelvic sidewall, taking seromuscular bites of the pouch and superficial bites of the pelvic sidewall, tying them together with several throws. This process is repeated on the patient's right side, ensuring minimal tension on the pouch. Two additional sutures are placed superior to the previous ones for reinforcement, avoiding vascular structures. The orientation and laxity of the J-pouch are assessed, with the uterus lifted to evaluate distal orientation and traction.

The surgeon places two pelvic drains in the surgical field, removes the trocars, and completes the procedure. The visual content shows the final steps of the surgery, ensuring proper drainage and closure.

When formulating a differential diagnosis in a patient with a J-pouch with outflow obstruction, you have to keep several key diagnoses in mind. J-pouch kinking or torsion, ilioanal stricture, intra-abdominal adhesions, and afferent loop obstruction. In this patient, we were able to rule out other pathologies and diagnose her with torsion. Laparoscopy in our patient proved to be an excellent diagnostic and therapeutic tool.

In conclusion, J-pouch torsion is an event that can be completely addressed laparoscopically with ease, and one should always be suspicious of torsion as a diagnosis if they present repeatedly for an anastomotic stricture dilation, but with continued outflow obstruction soon after.
